retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Bioceramic Sealers
retrieved_docs.content: Bioceramic sealers are biocompatible materials that use either calcium silicate or calcium phosphate as their main constituent. [21] Mineral trioxide aggregate (MTA) is a new material currently used as a root canal sealer, in addition to its many other uses within the field of endodontics. The formula of MTA is primarily tricalcium silicate with added bismuth oxide, a radiopaque powder for enhanced visualization on radiography. [21] [22] MTA is activated with the introduction of water and produces a highly alkaline mixture with a pH of 12 and beneficial calcium silicate hydrates. These hydrates make MTA an excellent endodontic material as they form on the surface of existing calcium silicate particles and continue to grow inwards, effectively creating a barrier. [21] [22] One major disadvantage of bioceramic sealers is that the enhanced adhesion between the material and the dentin wall makes endodontic retreatment and post-preparation increasingly challenging. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Apical Periodontitis -- Differential Diagnosis -- Apical Radiopacities
retrieved_docs.content: Hypercementosis Cemento-osseous dysplasia Idiopathic osteosclerosis Apical radiopacities are likely not associated with endodontic infection directly but are commonly found on routine radiographic evaluation. Radiopacities are generally benign.

retrieved_docs.title: Endodontic Materials Used To Fill Root Canals -- Properties -- Gutta-Percha
retrieved_docs.content: Gutta-percha has many advantages, such as ease of manipulation, radiopacity, low toxicity, and plasticity. However, it does not bond to dentin and shrinks when cooled. [5] [25] Gutta-percha cones are available in standardized 0.02 taper as specified by the International Organization of Standardization and non-standardized sizes. [5] This allows clinicians to mechanically prepare the canal space using various commercial rotary systems using standardized file sizes. When the canal is prepared to a given file size, a gutta-percha cone of the same size can be inserted and adapted to the canal. [1] [5] [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Dental Materials: Biodentine, a Calcium Silicate Bioactive -- Clinical Significance -- Clinical Applications of Biodentine: Furcation Perforation Repair
retrieved_docs.content: Biodentine is indicated as a material for furcation perforation repair. Furcation perforations are communications between the root canal system and the external dental surface in multirooted teeth. Repairing these perforations is a challenging aspect of dentistry. A study comparing two different dental repair materials, Biodentine and ProRoot MTA (Mineral Trioxide Aggregate), was conducted in dogs' teeth to treat furcation perforations. retrieved_docs.content: The intra-articular radiological calcification seen in patients with CPPD is characteristic. In knee x-rays, chondrocalcinosis presents as a thin dense intraarticular opacification that runs parallel and separated from the bone cortex or as a calcifying opacification in the menisci. [12] [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Calcium Pyrophosphate Deposition Disease -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: A number of specialists use the high-resolution US in the evaluation of patients with crystal arthropathies, as it may provide useful findings to help differentiate CPPD from other conditions such as gout. Chondrocalcinosis presents earlier in the US than in plain films. This modality may also find use in patients with symptomatic arthropathies of unclear etiology, or with inconclusive results in the fluid aspirate. Additionally, it could potentially decrease the need for using more expensive imaging modalities such as MRI. [16] [25] [26]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 291

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray is a fundamental radiographic examination that utilizes plain X‑ray techniques as its method of acquisition. Building on this basic imaging approach, more advanced studies are conceptually derived from the routine chest x-ray. A computerized tomography of chest is directly mapped from the routine chest x-ray as its primary extension, providing cross‑sectional detail of thoracic structures. Additionally, a computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium represents another, more comprehensive variant that is also mapped from the routine chest x-ray, offering combined neuro‑and thoracic evaluation with enhanced contrast visualization. This hierarchy illustrates how basic plain X‑ray methods underpin increasingly sophisticated CT imaging modalities, guiding clinical assessment and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2713
retrieved_docs.content: CT angiography of thorax with contrast and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are specific types of chest CT scans. Diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material, as well as diagnostic computed tomography of the thorax performed without contrast material followed by contrast material(s) and additional sections, represent narrower sub‑categories within the broader chest CT imaging modality. In addition, computed tomography of the chest is used as a finding method for detecting miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Together, these relationships delineate a hierarchical structure of chest CT techniques and illustrate how particular CT protocols are applied for detailed diagnostic evaluation of thoracic pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 448
retrieved_docs.content: as a child modality, indicating that tomography (including CT) is considered a specialized subcategory within the spectrum of plain radiographic techniques. This network of hierarchical, mapping, and metadata relationships integrates various CT examinations—both non‑contrast and contrast‑enhanced, single‑region and multi‑region—under unified anatomical and procedural categories, supporting consistent terminology and facilitating comprehensive patient care planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 4 -- Community 2899
retrieved_docs.content: Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium serves as the primary reference for the abbreviated term ct scan abdomen. The ct scan abdomen is clinically associated with تصوير البطن بالموجات فوق الصوتية (abdominal ultrasound imaging). Both imaging modalities are employed to evaluate abdominal pathology, and their clinical association reflects complementary roles in the diagnostic work‑up.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2463
retrieved_docs.content: CT of abdomen and pelvis and Computerized tomography of abdomen and pelvis with intravenous contrast medium are both classified as “Other CT scan,” indicating that each of these imaging procedures is considered an alternative or supplementary CT examination derived from a broader CT scan category.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2813
retrieved_docs.content: Ultrasonography of head and neck is classified as a form of diagnostic ultrasound of the head and neck.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
retrieved_docs.content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2519
retrieved_docs.content: Routine chest x-ray serves as a basic imaging modality, and both computerized tomography of brain and chest and computerized tomography of brain and chest with intravenous contrast medium are considered alternative or mapped imaging studies derived from the routine chest x-ray, indicating that these CT examinations may be employed for more detailed evaluation when the findings from a standard chest x-ray are insufficient.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2420
retrieved_docs.content: Arteriography is a diagnostic imaging technique that is used to visualize arteries, providing detailed images of arterial structures for clinical assessment and patient care.
